text: Approximately 44.7% of athletes have low energy availability (LEA), with similar prevalence in female (44.2%) and male (49.4%) athletes.

Approximately 63.0% of athletes are at risk of Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs), a more severe form of energy deficit affecting metabolic rate, menstrual function, immune function, bone health, protein synthesis, and cardiovascular function.

Athletes with low energy availability show decreased run performance, training response, endurance performance, coordination, concentration, judgment, explosive power, and agility compared to athletes with normal energy availability.
Low energy availability increases the likelihood of absence from training due to illness.
Athletes with low energy availability have impaired bone health and a higher risk of bone stress injuries.
Athletes in endurance, aesthetic, or weight-class sports are particularly at risk for low energy availability and REDs.
Early identification and correction of low energy availability can prevent REDs symptoms, reduce bone stress injury risk, and optimize training adaptations.
